questions.q: How would you secure a public-facing Web API?

answer.oneLiner: Securing a public API is about layering defenses so that if one control fails, another catches the attacker. You start at the identity layer — every endpoint must authenticate and authorize the caller, defaulting to deny — then you force HTTPS with HSTS, validate and encode all input and output, lock CORS to a strict whitelist, and add rate limiting plus security response headers. Finally, you put a WAF in front as a catch-all for known attack patterns, and you make sure database accounts and secrets follow least privilege so the blast radius of any breach is contained.
keyPoints: Authenticate and authorize every endpoint and deny by default, so a forgotten attribute fails closed rather than leaving an open endpoint.
Force HTTPS everywhere and add HSTS so browsers never attempt a plaintext connection to your API.
Validate all input server-side and encode all output — client-side validation is UX only, never a security control.
Lock CORS to a whitelist of specific origins rather than using a wildcard, and add rate limiting at both the gateway and app layers.
Add security response headers such as Content-Security-Policy and X-Content-Type-Options to harden browser behavior.
Apply least privilege to database accounts and secrets, and put a WAF in front as defense-in-depth against known attack patterns.

dialogue.text: We're exposing an internal Web API to the public internet. Where do you start?
I start with the perimeter and identity: force HTTPS/HSTS and make sure every endpoint authenticates and authorizes, deny by default.
Auth is in place. What else hardens it?
Validate all input server-side, lock CORS to a whitelist, add rate limiting, security headers, and put a WAF in front.
Suppose one endpoint is slow and gets hammered by a single client — where do you rate-limit, app or gateway?
Both ideally — coarse limits at the gateway to shed load early, finer per-user limits in the app where I know the identity.

followUpsQA.q: Where do you put rate limiting — app or gateway?
followUpsQA.a: Both: coarse at the edge/gateway, fine-grained per-identity in the app.
A forgotten `[Authorize]` then fails closed instead of leaking an open endpoint.

followUpsQA.q: Is HTTPS enough on its own?
followUpsQA.a: No — it only protects transit; you still need authN/Z, validation, and headers.

followUpsQA.q: What does a WAF add here?
followUpsQA.a: Edge filtering of known patterns (SQLi/XSS/bots) as defense-in-depth.

questions.q: What is CORS and how do you configure it securely in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: CORS is a browser-enforced mechanism that controls which external origins are allowed to call your API — it works by the browser checking response headers before allowing a cross-origin request to complete. By default browsers block cross-origin calls, and CORS is what you use to relax that restriction in a controlled way. In ASP.NET Core you configure it by registering a named policy that whitelists only the specific origins, methods, and headers your frontend needs, and you never combine a wildcard origin with credentials because that would let any website on the internet send your users' cookies to your API.
keyPoints: CORS is enforced by the browser, not the server — a non-browser client like Postman or curl ignores it entirely, so it is not a substitute for server-side authorization.
It relaxes the browser's same-origin policy, which by default blocks a page on one domain from making requests to a different domain.
In ASP.NET Core you use AddCors and UseCors with a named policy, and you whitelist only the specific origins, methods, and headers your app needs.

Never combine AllowAnyOrigin with AllowCredentials — ASP.NET Core actually throws an exception for this combination because it would allow any website to make authenticated requests using the user's cookies.

code.note: AllowAnyOrigin + AllowCredentials is invalid and exposes cookies/tokens.

redFlags: Calling CORS a server-side security control

dialogue.text: What problem does CORS actually solve?
It lets a browser relax the same-origin policy so a page from one origin can call your API on another — controlled by response headers.
How do you set it up safely in ASP.NET Core?
`AddCors` with a named policy plus `UseCors`, whitelisting specific origins rather than using a wildcard.
A dev sets `AllowAnyOrigin()` together with `AllowCredentials()` — what happens?
ASP.NET throws — that combo is illegal because it would let any site send the user's cookies; I'd name the exact origins instead.

followUpsQA.q: Does CORS protect the server itself?
followUpsQA.a: No — it's browser-enforced; a non-browser client ignores it entirely.
Wildcard origin + credentials would expose cookies to any site, so the spec forbids it.
Specific origins, methods, and headers — only what the client genuinely needs.

followUpsQA.q: Is CORS a substitute for authorization?
followUpsQA.a: No — it's a relaxation mechanism, not an access control.

questions.q: What is XSS and how do you prevent it?

answer.oneLiner: Cross-site scripting, or XSS, happens when an attacker manages to get malicious JavaScript to run inside another user's browser in the context of your website — for example, if a comment field stores a script tag that executes when other users view the page, the attacker can steal their session cookies or impersonate them. The core defense is context-aware output encoding, which means you encode user-supplied text differently depending on where it appears in the page — in HTML, in an attribute, in JavaScript, or in a URL — so the browser always treats it as data rather than executable code. You layer on a Content-Security-Policy header to restrict which script sources the browser is allowed to run, and you mark session cookies as HttpOnly so that even if XSS slips through, injected scripts cannot read them.
keyPoints: There are three main variants: stored XSS where the payload is saved in the database and served to all users, reflected XSS where it comes back in the response from a URL parameter, and DOM-based XSS where the payload never reaches the server but gets injected into the page entirely in the browser.

The correct fix is context-aware output encoding — HTML-encode when writing into HTML, attribute-encode for HTML attributes, JavaScript-encode for script contexts — because simply blocking script tags misses dozens of other injection vectors.
Razor in ASP.NET Core encodes output by default, which is why you should avoid Html.Raw on any untrusted data since it bypasses that protection entirely.
A Content-Security-Policy header restricts which script sources the browser will load, so even if encoding is missed somewhere, injected or inline scripts are blocked.
Mark session cookies as HttpOnly so that even a successful XSS attack cannot read the cookie and hijack the session.

dialogue.text: Explain XSS in one breath.
It's getting attacker-controlled script to run in another user's browser in the context of your site.
How do you prevent it in a .NET app?
Context-aware output encoding — Razor auto-encodes — plus a Content-Security-Policy, and I avoid `Html.Raw` on untrusted data.
What about script that never touches the server, built purely in the browser from the URL?
That's DOM-based XSS; I encode/sanitize in the client sink too and avoid `innerHTML` with untrusted data, since server encoding alone won't catch it.

followUpsQA.a: XSS where untrusted data reaches a client-side sink like `innerHTML` without ever round-tripping the server.

followUpsQA.q: How does CSP reduce XSS impact?
followUpsQA.a: It restricts which script sources can run, blocking injected/inline scripts even if encoding is missed.
It stops injected script from reading the session cookie, limiting the damage.
There are countless injection contexts; encoding for the output context is the real fix.

questions.q: What is CSRF and how do you prevent it?

answer.oneLiner: Cross-site request forgery tricks a logged-in user's browser into sending a request to your site that the user never intended — for example, a malicious page might embed an image tag whose URL is your bank's transfer endpoint, and the browser silently attaches the user's session cookie when loading it. The attack works because browsers automatically include cookies on any matching request, regardless of which page triggered it. You prevent it by including an antiforgery token — a secret value the malicious page cannot read — in every state-changing request, and by setting your cookies with SameSite=Lax or Strict so the browser refuses to send them on cross-site requests in the first place.
keyPoints: CSRF only affects authentication mechanisms where the browser automatically attaches credentials, which means session cookies — APIs that use a Bearer token in the Authorization header are largely immune because the browser does not add that header automatically on cross-site requests.

The antiforgery token defense works by embedding a secret value in the page that the server issued — a cross-site attacker cannot read it due to the same-origin policy, so they cannot forge a valid request.
In ASP.NET Core you protect state-changing actions with the ValidateAntiForgeryToken attribute, which automatically validates the token on POST, PUT, and DELETE requests.
Setting cookies with SameSite=Lax prevents the browser from sending them on cross-site requests at all, which is the most modern and effective defense and works alongside antiforgery tokens.
HTTPS does not prevent CSRF — it only encrypts the traffic in transit, but the attack relies on the browser sending the cookie, not on anyone reading the wire.

dialogue.text: What is CSRF?
It tricks a logged-in user's browser into firing an authenticated request to your site that the user never intended.
Why does it work, and how do you stop it?
It works because the browser auto-attaches cookies; I stop it with antiforgery tokens and `SameSite=Lax/Strict` cookies.
Our SPA calls the API with a Bearer token in the Authorization header — are we still exposed?
Largely no — the browser doesn't auto-send Authorization headers, so a cross-site request can't forge it; CSRF mainly bites cookie-based auth.

followUpsQA.q: Why are pure JWT-in-header APIs less exposed?
followUpsQA.a: Browsers don't auto-attach Authorization headers cross-site, so the request can't be forged.
Lax allows top-level navigation GETs; Strict blocks all cross-site sends — Strict is safer but can break links.
No — HTTPS protects transit, not the user's intent.
On state-changing requests (POST/PUT/DELETE), validated server-side.

questions.q: How do you prevent SQL injection?

answer.oneLiner: SQL injection happens when an attacker puts SQL code into a form field or URL parameter and your app blindly includes that text in a database query — for example, entering a value like ' OR '1'='1 into a login box can bypass authentication entirely if the query is built by string concatenation. The fix is to use parameterized queries or prepared statements, which tell the database driver to always treat user input as data rather than part of the SQL command itself. Entity Framework's LINQ queries are parameterized by default, which is one reason using an ORM correctly is safer than writing raw SQL strings.
keyPoints: Never build SQL strings by concatenating user input directly — even something as simple as a search box becomes an injection point if the input is not parameterized.
Parameterized commands and Entity Framework LINQ queries send the user input separately from the SQL command, so the database engine cannot interpret it as code.

In Entity Framework, FromSqlInterpolated is safe because it parameterizes the C# interpolation automatically, but FromSqlRaw with a concatenated string is just as dangerous as raw ADO.NET string building.
Use a least-privilege database account for the application — if the account cannot drop tables or access other schemas, a successful injection does far less damage.
Input validation and a WAF are useful as defense-in-depth but they are not substitutes for parameterization, which is the actual fix.

code.note: FromSqlRaw with string concatenation is NOT safe.

dialogue.text: How do you prevent SQL injection?
Never concatenate user input into SQL — use parameterized commands, or EF which parameterizes for you.
In EF specifically, what's the safe vs unsafe raw call?
`FromSqlInterpolated` is safe because it parameterizes the interpolation; `FromSqlRaw` with a concatenated string is not.
Say injection still slips through somewhere — what limits the blast radius?
A least-privilege DB account — if the app user can't drop tables or read other schemas, a successful injection does far less damage.

followUpsQA.q: Is EF immune to SQL injection?
followUpsQA.a: LINQ is parameterized, but `FromSqlRaw` with string concat reopens the hole.

followUpsQA.q: What does least privilege add here?
followUpsQA.a: It caps the damage if injection succeeds — no db_owner, only needed CRUD.

followUpsQA.q: Why isn't input validation enough alone?
followUpsQA.a: It's defense-in-depth; parameterization is the actual fix.

followUpsQA.q: Safe way to run raw SQL in EF?
followUpsQA.a: `FromSqlInterpolated` / parameter objects, never concatenated strings.

questions.q: What is over-posting / mass assignment and how do you prevent it?

answer.oneLiner: Over-posting, also called mass assignment, is a vulnerability where a client sends extra fields in a request body that your app was never intended to accept, and the model binder quietly applies them to your object — the classic example is a user posting IsAdmin=true to a profile-update endpoint that binds directly to the user entity, accidentally granting themselves administrator access. The fix is to bind incoming requests to a purpose-built data transfer object that only contains the fields you actually want to accept, and then map that DTO to your entity explicitly on the server side. This approach is inherently safe because any field not on the DTO is simply ignored, regardless of what the client sends.
keyPoints: The vulnerability happens when your controller action binds the request body directly to a domain entity or database model that contains fields the client should never be able to set, such as roles, admin flags, or audit fields.
The correct fix is to use an input DTO that only declares the fields you want to accept — anything the client sends that is not on the DTO is automatically ignored by the model binder.
You then map the DTO to the entity explicitly on the server side, copying only the intended fields, which keeps control in your hands.

Using the Bind attribute on an entity to restrict which fields are bound is fragile — if someone adds a privileged property to the entity later, it silently becomes bindable, whereas a DTO is safe by construction.
The BindNever attribute can mark individual properties on an entity to exclude them from binding, but a DTO is the preferred approach for any endpoint that handles sensitive data.

code.note: Never bind directly to an entity that has privileged fields.

redFlags: Binding requests straight to EF entities

dialogue.text: What is over-posting, or mass assignment?
It's when a client sends extra fields you didn't intend to accept — like `IsAdmin` — and model binding quietly sets them.
How do you prevent it?
Bind to a purpose-built input DTO that only contains allowed fields, then map to the entity explicitly server-side.
A teammate prefers `[Bind]` on the entity instead of a DTO — your take?
It works but it's fragile; a new privileged property silently becomes bindable, whereas a DTO is allow-list by construction.

followUpsQA.a: Marks a property so model binding never populates it from the request.
DTOs are allow-list by design; `[Bind]` lists can drift as the entity grows.

followUpsQA.q: Where do you map DTO to entity?
followUpsQA.a: Explicitly server-side, copying only the intended fields.
Posting `IsAdmin=true` to a profile-update endpoint bound to the entity.

questions.q: Which security response headers matter and what do they do?

answer.oneLiner: Security response headers are instructions your server sends to the browser telling it how to behave when rendering your pages, and a small set of them collectively close off a wide range of client-side attacks. The most important ones are Content-Security-Policy to restrict which scripts and resources the browser will load, X-Content-Type-Options to prevent the browser from guessing at content types and accidentally executing a file as a script, X-Frame-Options to stop your pages being embedded in an attacker's iframe for clickjacking, and Strict-Transport-Security to tell browsers to always use HTTPS for your domain. You should also set Referrer-Policy to control how much URL information leaks to third parties, and Permissions-Policy to disable browser features your app does not use.
keyPoints: Content-Security-Policy is the most powerful header — it tells the browser which domains are allowed to supply scripts, styles, images, and other resources, which is the best mitigation against XSS after encoding. It requires careful tuning to avoid breaking your app.
X-Content-Type-Options set to nosniff stops the browser from second-guessing the Content-Type you declared — without it, a browser might execute a text file as a script if it looks like JavaScript.

X-Frame-Options set to DENY prevents your page from being embedded in any iframe, closing off clickjacking attacks. The modern equivalent is the frame-ancestors directive in your Content-Security-Policy.

Strict-Transport-Security tells the browser to always use HTTPS for your domain for a specified period, eliminating the first unencrypted request. Be careful not to enable it in local development, because it gets cached and will prevent HTTP access even on localhost.
Referrer-Policy controls how much of your URL the browser includes in the Referer header on outgoing requests, which can leak sensitive path information to third parties.
Permissions-Policy lets you disable browser features your app does not need, such as the camera, microphone, or geolocation, reducing the surface area if XSS does occur.

code.note: CSP is the strongest but needs tuning to avoid breaking the app.

dialogue.text: Which security response headers do you reach for first?
CSP, `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `X-Frame-Options`, HSTS, plus Referrer-Policy and Permissions-Policy.
What does CSP buy you over the others?
It's the strongest XSS mitigation — it restricts which script and resource sources the browser will load, but it needs tuning.
You enable HSTS and your team can't reach the site on `localhost` over HTTP anymore — what happened?
HSTS got cached and forces HTTPS even in dev; I keep `UseHsts` for production only and exclude localhost so dev isn't pinned to HTTPS.

followUpsQA.q: Which header replaces X-Frame-Options in modern CSP?
followUpsQA.a: `frame-ancestors` in the Content-Security-Policy.
It hardcodes HTTPS-only into browsers, removing the first insecure request.
The browser guessing content types and executing, say, a script served as text.

followUpsQA.q: Why not ship CSP with unsafe-inline everywhere?
followUpsQA.a: It defeats CSP's main purpose by re-allowing inline injected script.

questions.q: What is clickjacking and how do you defend against it?

answer.oneLiner: Clickjacking is an attack where an attacker loads your website invisibly inside an iframe on their own malicious page, then overlays it with fake UI so that when a user thinks they are clicking something innocent, they are actually clicking a button on your site — for example, confirming a bank transfer or changing their password. The fix is straightforward: tell the browser your page is not allowed to be embedded in any iframe, which you do with the X-Frame-Options response header set to DENY, or with the frame-ancestors directive in your Content-Security-Policy. You should set both for the widest browser support.
keyPoints: The attack works by loading your site in a transparent or hidden iframe over an attacker-controlled page, so the user's clicks land on your page without them realizing it.

Setting X-Frame-Options to DENY prevents any origin from embedding your page in an iframe. Using SAMEORIGIN instead allows your own domain to embed it, which is useful if your own app legitimately uses iframes.

The Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directive is the modern equivalent and is more flexible — you can specify exactly which origins are allowed to frame your content, and it overrides X-Frame-Options in browsers that support it.
You should set both headers for the broadest browser compatibility, since older browsers may not support the CSP directive.
JavaScript-based frame-busting scripts are not a reliable defense because they can be bypassed by the attacker sandboxing the iframe — rely on the headers.
redFlags: No framing protection on sensitive pages

dialogue.text: What's clickjacking?
An attacker loads your page invisibly in an iframe and overlays it, so the user's clicks hit your site without realizing it.
How do you defend against it?
Refuse to be framed — `X-Frame-Options: DENY` and the modern CSP `frame-ancestors 'none'`.
Your app embeds its own dashboards in its own pages — does DENY break that?
Yes — I'd use `SAMEORIGIN` / `frame-ancestors 'self'` so my own origin can frame it but no one else can.

followUpsQA.a: Same goal; `frame-ancestors` is the modern, more flexible CSP directive — set both for coverage.
When your own pages legitimately frame your own content.
Sensitive, state-changing pages like settings or payments.
No — it's bypassable; rely on the headers.

questions.q: What is a WAF and what does it protect against?

answer.oneLiner: A Web Application Firewall sits in front of your application at the network edge and inspects incoming HTTP traffic, blocking requests that match known attack patterns like SQL injection payloads, XSS strings, and malicious bot signatures before they even reach your app. It is an important layer of defense-in-depth — think of it as a filter that catches known-bad traffic and buys you time against zero-day exploits — but it is not a replacement for fixing vulnerabilities in your own code. A WAF cannot stop business-logic abuse where an attacker sends perfectly valid requests that exploit a flaw in how your application behaves.
keyPoints: A WAF operates at the HTTP layer at the network edge, typically via a service like Azure Front Door, Azure Application Gateway, or Cloudflare, and blocks requests that match known attack signatures before they reach your application servers.

It uses rule sets such as the OWASP Core Rule Set to identify common attack patterns including SQL injection, cross-site scripting, and known vulnerability probes, and it can enforce rate limits and geo or IP-based filters.

A WAF is defense-in-depth — it reduces attack surface and provides an early warning layer, but it does not replace secure coding practices because an attacker who crafts a payload the rules do not recognize will still get through.

It cannot protect against business-logic vulnerabilities because those involve valid-looking requests that your application processes incorrectly — for example, a coupon-drain attack using a legitimate checkout endpoint.
A WAF left in detection-only mode logs attacks but does not block them — you need to enable prevention mode for it to actually protect your application.
redFlags: Treating a WAF as full protection

dialogue.text: What is a WAF and what does it do for you?
A Web Application Firewall sits at the edge and inspects HTTP traffic, blocking known attack patterns like SQLi, XSS, and bad bots.
So can we skip fixing the code if we have one?
No — it's defense-in-depth that buys time against known patterns and zero-days, but it never replaces secure code.
An attacker abuses a legit checkout endpoint to drain a coupon — does the WAF catch it?
No — that's business-logic abuse with valid-looking requests; WAFs catch malicious patterns, not flawed logic, so I'd fix the rule server-side.

followUpsQA.q: Can a WAF stop business-logic abuse?
followUpsQA.a: No — valid-looking requests pass; logic flaws must be fixed in the app.

followUpsQA.q: What is the OWASP Core Rule Set?
followUpsQA.a: A standard set of generic WAF rules covering common attack classes.
At the edge — Azure Front Door/App Gateway, Cloudflare, etc.
Then it logs attacks but doesn't block them — prevention mode is the point.

questions.q: How do you handle input validation in a Web API?

answer.oneLiner: Every piece of data entering your API must be validated on the server, regardless of what any client-side form did first — a determined attacker will bypass your frontend entirely and call your endpoint directly. The correct mindset is an allow-list approach: define exactly what values, formats, and ranges you expect, and reject anything that does not match rather than trying to enumerate all the bad inputs. In ASP.NET Core, the ApiController attribute gives you automatic model validation out of the box, and you can add more expressive rules with data annotations or a library like FluentValidation.
keyPoints: Client-side validation is a user experience feature, not a security control — anyone can send a raw HTTP request directly to your API and bypass any JavaScript validation entirely.
Use an allow-list mindset: define what good input looks like and reject anything else, rather than trying to block specific bad characters or patterns which attackers can work around.
The ApiController attribute in ASP.NET Core automatically checks the model state and returns a 400 Bad Request response before your action method even runs if the model is invalid.
You can add validation rules with data annotations such as Required, MaxLength, and Range on your DTO properties, or use FluentValidation for more complex rules.
You should validate input even from authenticated internal services — authentication proves identity, not that the payload is well-formed, and internal services can be compromised or have bugs.

dialogue.text: How do you handle input validation in a Web API?
Validate everything on the server with an allow-list mindset — define expected formats and ranges and reject anything else.
What does ASP.NET give you out of the box?
`[ApiController]` auto-checks `ModelState`, and I layer data annotations or FluentValidation on top.
The caller is an authenticated internal service — can you trust its payload?
No — I still validate; internal callers get compromised or buggy too, and validation is cheap insurance.

followUpsQA.a: It's only UX — anyone can bypass it with a direct request.
Allow-list accepts only known-good; deny-list always misses new bad inputs.
The `[ApiController]` attribute returns 400 on invalid models automatically.
Yes — auth proves identity, not that the payload is well-formed.

questions.q: Walk me through the OWASP Top 10 at a high level.

answer.oneLiner: The OWASP Top 10 is a regularly updated list of the most critical web application security risks, ordered by how commonly they appear in real-world applications and how severe the impact is. The current list is led by Broken Access Control, meaning users reaching data or performing actions they should not be allowed to — this overtook injection because access control failures are now the most widespread finding. Other prominent entries include Cryptographic Failures where sensitive data is not properly encrypted, Injection including SQL and command injection, Security Misconfiguration, and newer entries like Server-Side Request Forgery which is particularly common in cloud-hosted applications. It is a prioritization and awareness tool, not a complete security checklist.
keyPoints: Broken Access Control is the current number one risk, covering things like insecure direct object references where a user can access another user's data by changing an ID in the URL, or missing authorization checks on admin endpoints.
Cryptographic Failures covers cases where sensitive data such as passwords or personal information is transmitted or stored without adequate encryption, or where weak or outdated algorithms are used.
Injection covers SQL injection, command injection, and similar attacks where untrusted data is included in a query or command that gets executed.
Security Misconfiguration is one of the most common findings — things like default credentials left in place, unnecessary features enabled, or error messages that reveal too much internal detail.

Server-Side Request Forgery is where your application fetches a URL that an attacker controls, which can expose internal services, cloud metadata endpoints, or other internal infrastructure that should not be reachable from the outside.
The Top 10 is a prioritization tool based on real-world data — it helps teams focus on the most impactful risks first, but it does not cover every possible vulnerability.
redFlags: Naming only injection/XSS and nothing about access control

dialogue.text: Give me a high-level tour of the OWASP Top 10.
It's OWASP's prioritized list of the most critical web risks, currently led by Broken Access Control, then Cryptographic Failures and Injection.
Why is access control number one?
Because it's the most common and impactful — users reaching data or actions they shouldn't, like IDOR or missing role checks.
One entry is SSRF — what is it and why did it make the list?
Server-Side Request Forgery: the app fetches a URL an attacker controls, often hitting internal services or cloud metadata; it's common in modern cloud apps.

followUpsQA.a: Broken Access Control — most prevalent and high-impact (users acting beyond their permissions).
Tricking the server into fetching attacker-chosen URLs, often internal/metadata endpoints.

followUpsQA.q: Is the Top 10 a complete checklist?
followUpsQA.a: No — it's awareness and prioritization, not exhaustive coverage.
Still top-tier but below access control and crypto failures in the current list.

questions.q: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

answer.oneLiner: Authentication is the process of proving who you are — logging in with a password or presenting a valid token — while authorization is the separate decision of what you are allowed to do once your identity is known. They are distinct steps that always happen in that order: you cannot make an access decision without first knowing who is asking. In ASP.NET Core this is reflected in the middleware pipeline where UseAuthentication must come before UseAuthorization.
keyPoints: Authentication answers the question 'who are you?' and is typically handled through credentials like a username and password, a token, or a certificate.
Authorization answers the question 'are you allowed to do this?' and evaluates the authenticated identity against roles, claims, or policies.
Authentication always happens before authorization because you need to establish identity before you can make any access decision.
In ASP.NET Core, UseAuthentication must be called before UseAuthorization in the middleware pipeline — reversing them means the user principal is not populated when authorization runs.

A valid token that is rejected by an authorization check returns a 403 Forbidden response, while a missing or invalid token returns a 401 Unauthorized — these two HTTP status codes represent failures at different stages.

dialogue.text: Authentication vs authorization — what's the difference?
Authentication proves who you are; authorization decides what you're allowed to do.
Which happens first in ASP.NET Core, and how is that wired?
Authentication first — `UseAuthentication()` must come before `UseAuthorization()` so the user is known before we check permissions.
If someone presents a valid token but hits an admin-only endpoint, which one fails?
Authentication passes — we know who they are — but authorization fails and returns 403, versus a 401 for no/invalid identity.

followUpsQA.q: Which middleware comes first and why?
followUpsQA.a: `UseAuthentication` before `UseAuthorization` — you must know the user before checking permissions.
AuthN: logging in with a password. AuthZ: only admins can delete users.
401 = not authenticated; 403 = authenticated but not allowed.
No — you need an identity to evaluate permissions against.

questions.q: What's the difference between OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect?

answer.oneLiner: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework designed to let a user grant a third-party application limited access to their resources — it answers 'can this app call this API on the user's behalf?' and issues an access token for that purpose. OpenID Connect is a thin identity layer built on top of OAuth 2.0 that adds the concept of who the user actually is, issuing an ID token that contains claims like the user's name and email address so the application can sign them in. The key distinction is that OAuth alone is about authorization and API access, while OpenID Connect adds authentication and is what you should use whenever you need to log a user into your application.
keyPoints: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework, not an authentication protocol — it grants access tokens that allow an application to call APIs on a user's behalf, but those tokens do not necessarily tell you who the user is.

OpenID Connect adds an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 by issuing an ID token, which is a JSON Web Token containing verified claims about the user such as their subject identifier, email, and name.
The ID token is what you use to sign a user into your application, while the access token is what you present to an API to prove you are authorized to call it.
A common mistake is using an access token to identify the user — access tokens are opaque or may lack user claims and are intended for API authorization, not as a user identity source.
Use OpenID Connect whenever you need to authenticate a user and know who they are. Use OAuth 2.0 alone for machine-to-machine scenarios where no human identity is involved.
redFlags: Using an access token to identify the user

dialogue.text: OAuth 2.0 versus OpenID Connect — clear it up for me.
OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that issues access tokens for calling APIs; OIDC is an identity layer on top that adds an ID token to authenticate the user.
So what's actually in each token?
The access token grants API access; the ID token is a JWT describing who the user is — claims like `sub` and `email`.
A junior uses the access token to populate the user's profile name — what's wrong?
Access tokens are for authorization, not identity — they may be opaque or lack user claims; the ID token (or userinfo endpoint) is the right source.

followUpsQA.q: What's in an ID token vs an access token?
followUpsQA.a: ID token = who the user is (claims); access token = permission to call an API.

followUpsQA.q: When do you only need OAuth?
followUpsQA.a: Delegated API access where you don't need to identify the end user.
No — that's OIDC; OAuth alone is authorization.

followUpsQA.q: What format is an ID token?
followUpsQA.a: A JWT with user identity claims.

questions.q: What is the PKCE flow and why is it used for SPAs and mobile apps?

answer.oneLiner: PKCE, which stands for Proof Key for Code Exchange, is an extension to the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow that solves the problem of public clients — applications like single-page apps and mobile apps that cannot safely store a client secret because their code is visible to anyone. The way it works is that the client generates a random secret called a code verifier before starting the login flow, hashes it to produce a code challenge, and sends the hash to the authorization server upfront. When the authorization code comes back and the client exchanges it for tokens, it must prove it knows the original verifier — so even if someone intercepts the authorization code, they cannot use it without that verifier.
keyPoints: The problem PKCE solves is that public clients like SPAs and native apps cannot keep a client secret — anything embedded in a JavaScript bundle or a mobile app binary can be extracted by an attacker.

PKCE replaces the client secret with a per-request proof: the client generates a random code verifier, hashes it to produce a code challenge, and sends only the hash to the authorization server at the start of the flow.

When the client later presents the authorization code to exchange it for tokens, it must also present the original code verifier — the server hashes it and checks it matches the challenge it stored, proving the client is the same one that started the flow.
This means an intercepted authorization code is useless to an attacker because they do not have the code verifier that the legitimate client generated in memory.

PKCE replaces the older implicit flow, which returned tokens directly in the URL fragment with no proof of possession and was deprecated because those tokens could be intercepted from browser history, referrer headers, or logging.
redFlags: Still using implicit flow for SPAs
Embedding a client secret in a SPA

dialogue.text: What is PKCE and why does it exist?
It's the Authorization Code flow plus a one-time `code_verifier`/`code_challenge`, so public clients that can't keep a secret can still exchange codes securely.
Walk me through how the challenge protects the exchange.
The client sends a hashed `code_challenge` up front, then proves possession with the original `code_verifier` at token exchange — an intercepted code is useless without it.
Why not just embed a client secret in the SPA instead?
A SPA's bundle is public, so any 'secret' is extractable; PKCE replaces the secret with a per-request proof, which is why implicit flow was deprecated.

followUpsQA.q: Why was the implicit flow deprecated?
followUpsQA.a: It returned tokens in the URL fragment with no proof-of-possession — leaky and replayable.
In the client's memory/session for the duration of the flow, never sent until exchange.
Public clients — SPAs, mobile, native apps that can't store a secret.

followUpsQA.q: Does PKCE need a client secret?
followUpsQA.a: No — the verifier replaces it.

questions.q: Describe the structure of a JWT and how you validate one.

answer.oneLiner: A JSON Web Token consists of three base64url-encoded sections separated by dots: a header that declares the signing algorithm, a payload that contains the claims such as the user's identity and when the token expires, and a signature that the issuer computed over the first two sections. The payload is only encoded, not encrypted, so anyone can read it — you should never put sensitive data like passwords or internal IDs in it. When you receive a JWT you must validate the signature against the issuer's public keys, confirm the issuer and audience match what you expect, and check that the token has not expired before trusting any of its claims.
keyPoints: A JWT has three dot-separated parts: the header specifying the algorithm, the payload containing the claims, and the cryptographic signature over the first two parts.
The payload is base64url-encoded, which means it is trivially decodable by anyone — do not put secrets, passwords, or sensitive business data in the JWT claims.
When validating a JWT you must check the signature against the identity provider's published public signing keys, which are available at a well-known JWKS endpoint.

You must also validate the issuer claim to confirm the token came from the expected identity provider, the audience claim to confirm it was issued for your specific API, and the expiry and not-before timestamps.

Never accept a token with the algorithm set to none — this is a well-known attack where an attacker strips the signature and sets the algorithm to none, then presents forged claims with no cryptographic proof.

code.note: Disabling any of these validations is a common, serious bug.

dialogue.text: Describe a JWT's structure.
Three base64url parts: a header with the algorithm, a payload of claims, and a signature over the first two.
Is the payload safe to put a secret in?
No — it's encoded, not encrypted; anyone can decode it, so it must never hold secrets.
A token arrives with `alg: none` and no signature — do you accept it?
Never — that's the classic bypass; I validate signature, `iss`, `aud`, `exp`/`nbf` against the IdP's JWKS keys and reject unsigned tokens.

followUpsQA.q: Why can't you store secrets in the payload?
followUpsQA.a: It's base64-encoded, not encrypted — trivially readable by anyone.
The IdP's published set of public signing keys used to verify the JWT signature.
Signature, issuer, audience, and lifetime (`exp`/`nbf`).
It means unsigned — an attacker could forge any claims.

questions.q: JWT vs cookie authentication — when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: Cookie-based authentication works well for traditional server-rendered web applications because the browser handles the cookie automatically, and marking it HttpOnly prevents JavaScript from reading it which reduces XSS risk — but it introduces CSRF exposure because the browser sends the cookie on every matching request including cross-site ones. JWTs sent in the Authorization header work better for APIs, single-page apps, and mobile clients because they work seamlessly across domains and the browser does not automatically attach them to cross-site requests, making them immune to CSRF. The main downside of JWTs is that they are hard to revoke before they expire, which is why the common pattern is a short-lived access token combined with a long-lived refresh token stored in an HttpOnly cookie.
keyPoints: Cookie authentication is ideal for traditional web apps — the browser attaches the cookie automatically on every request, it can be marked HttpOnly to prevent JavaScript from reading it, but it requires CSRF protection because browsers send cookies on cross-site requests too.

JWT authentication is better for APIs, SPAs, and mobile apps where you need to call multiple domains or services, because the token is sent explicitly in the Authorization header and the browser does not auto-attach it cross-site.

The main downside of JWTs is revocation difficulty — once issued, a JWT remains valid until it expires, so there is no built-in way to invalidate it immediately if a user logs out or a token is compromised.

Storing a JWT in localStorage is convenient but exposes it to any JavaScript running on the page, including injected scripts — an HttpOnly cookie or in-memory storage with a secure refresh mechanism is safer.

The hybrid pattern combines the strengths of both: a short-lived JWT access token for API calls, plus a long-lived refresh token stored in an HttpOnly cookie, rotated on each use so that a stolen refresh token can be detected.
redFlags: Storing JWTs in localStorage without considering XSS
Long-lived JWTs with no revocation plan

dialogue.text: JWT versus cookie authentication — when each?
Cookies fit server-rendered apps and are XSS-safer when HttpOnly but CSRF-prone; JWTs fit APIs/SPAs/mobile and cross-domain calls but need careful storage.
Where should a SPA actually store a JWT, then?
Ideally not in `localStorage` due to XSS — an HttpOnly cookie or in-memory with a refresh token is safer.
You need cross-domain API calls and easy revocation — what do you reach for?
The hybrid: a short-lived JWT access token for the API plus a refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie, rotated each use, so I get statelessness and a revocation point.

followUpsQA.q: Where should a SPA store a JWT?
followUpsQA.a: Not `localStorage` — prefer HttpOnly cookie or in-memory to limit XSS theft.

followUpsQA.q: How does the hybrid pattern work?
followUpsQA.a: Short-lived JWT access token + refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie, rotated on use.
CSRF — needs antiforgery tokens / SameSite.
Hard to revoke before expiry.

questions.q: How do refresh tokens work and where should they be stored?

answer.oneLiner: A refresh token is a long-lived credential that the client uses to obtain new short-lived access tokens without asking the user to log in again — this lets you keep access tokens very short-lived, typically just minutes, which limits the damage if one leaks. Because the refresh token itself is long-lived and powerful, it must be stored very carefully: for web apps it belongs in an HttpOnly and Secure cookie so JavaScript cannot read it, and on mobile it goes in platform-provided secure storage. To detect theft, you rotate the refresh token on every use and implement reuse detection — if an already-used refresh token is presented again, you know it was stolen and you revoke the entire token family for that user.
keyPoints: Refresh tokens exist so that access tokens can be kept very short-lived — typically a few minutes — because the shorter the access token lifetime, the smaller the window if it is stolen.
On every use, the refresh token should be rotated: the server issues a new refresh token and immediately invalidates the old one. This limits how long a stolen refresh token remains usable.

Reuse detection is the mechanism for catching stolen tokens: if a client presents a refresh token that has already been used and replaced, the server knows that either the legitimate client or an attacker replayed it, and the safe response is to revoke the entire token family and force the user to log in again.
For web applications, store the refresh token in an HttpOnly and Secure cookie — this prevents JavaScript from ever reading it, so XSS attacks cannot steal it.
Never store a refresh token in localStorage or sessionStorage — these are accessible to any JavaScript on the page, making them vulnerable to XSS.

dialogue.text: How do refresh tokens work?
A refresh token is a long-lived credential you exchange for new short-lived access tokens, so access tokens can expire in minutes.
Where do you store one safely?
Server-side or in an HttpOnly+Secure cookie for web, secure storage on mobile — never in `localStorage`.
How would you catch a refresh token that's been stolen and replayed?
Rotation with reuse detection: each refresh issues a new token and invalidates the old; if an already-used token reappears, I revoke the whole token family.

followUpsQA.a: Issuing a new refresh token on each use and invalidating the previous one.

followUpsQA.q: How do you detect a stolen refresh token?
followUpsQA.a: Reuse detection — a replayed old token signals theft, so revoke the family.
HttpOnly+Secure cookie (web) or platform secure storage (mobile).
They limit the window if one leaks, since revocation is hard.

questions.q: Compare RBAC, claims-based, and policy-based authorization in ASP.NET Core.

answer.oneLiner: Role-based access control is the simplest model — you decorate an endpoint with the roles that are allowed to call it, and the framework checks whether the user has one of those roles. Claims-based authorization is more granular — instead of a broad role you check for a specific claim value like a department or subscription tier. Policy-based authorization is the most flexible and is what ASP.NET Core recommends as the default: you define named, reusable rules that can combine role checks, claim checks, and arbitrarily complex custom logic, then reference those policy names on your endpoints — this keeps authorization logic centralized and avoids scattering hardcoded role strings across the codebase.
keyPoints: Role-based access control, or RBAC, assigns users to roles and checks whether the user holds a required role on the endpoint. It is simple but coarse-grained — a user is either in the Admin role or they are not, with no room for nuance.

Claims-based authorization checks whether the user's identity token contains a specific claim with a specific value, which allows finer-grained decisions such as allowing only users with a department claim of HR to access payroll endpoints.

Policy-based authorization wraps both of these into named, centrally-defined rules using the AddAuthorization registration in Program.cs, so the authorization logic lives in one place rather than being repeated as role strings on every attribute.

You can write a custom IAuthorizationRequirement and handler for logic that cannot be expressed with roles or claims alone — for example, allowing only the manager of the same department as the resource being accessed.

Prefer policies over raw role checks in general — if the role logic ever needs to change, you update it in one place in the policy definition rather than finding every Authorize attribute across the application.

code.note: Roles are really just claims under the hood.

dialogue.text: Compare RBAC, claims-based, and policy-based authorization.
RBAC checks roles, claims-based checks specific claim values, and policy-based wraps both plus custom logic into named, reusable rules.
Which do you default to and why?
Policy-based — it's the most flexible and centralizes the rule, so I'm not scattering role-string checks everywhere.
You need 'manager of the same department as the record' — which approach?
A custom policy with an `IAuthorizationRequirement` and handler, because that's resource-and-relationship logic roles or single claims can't express.

followUpsQA.q: When would you write a custom requirement handler?
followUpsQA.a: For logic beyond roles/claims — e.g. resource ownership or relational checks.
Named, reusable, centralized rules instead of scattered hardcoded role strings.
Yes — roles are essentially a kind of claim under the hood.
It's coarse-grained — fine for simple admin/user, weak for nuanced rules.

questions.q: What are Azure AD / Entra ID and Managed Identity, and how do they help?

answer.oneLiner: Entra ID, which Microsoft previously called Azure Active Directory, is Microsoft's cloud identity platform — it handles user sign-in via standard protocols like OpenID Connect and OAuth 2.0, issues tokens, manages groups and roles, and supports enterprise federation. Managed Identity is a related feature that gives an Azure resource such as an App Service or a virtual machine its own identity in Entra ID, so the resource can authenticate to other Azure services like Key Vault or SQL without any stored credentials — the platform issues and rotates the token automatically, completely removing the need to manage a secret. This combination means you can run your application in Azure with no passwords or connection string secrets in your code or configuration files.
keyPoints: Entra ID is Microsoft's cloud identity provider that handles user and application authentication using OpenID Connect and OAuth 2.0, issuing ID tokens and access tokens for your applications.

Managed Identity gives an Azure resource its own identity in Entra ID without requiring you to create or manage any credentials — you simply assign the identity to the resource and then grant it the permissions it needs.

The platform automatically issues and rotates the authentication token for a Managed Identity, so there is no password, connection string, or service principal secret that could be accidentally committed to source control or exposed in a breach.

System-assigned Managed Identity is tied to the lifecycle of a single Azure resource and is deleted when that resource is deleted. User-assigned Managed Identity is a standalone resource that can be shared across multiple Azure services and has an independent lifecycle.

You use Managed Identity together with Azure RBAC to grant the identity only the specific permissions it needs — for example, read access to a specific Key Vault secret rather than access to the whole subscription.
redFlags: Storing service principal secrets when MI is available

dialogue.text: What are Entra ID and Managed Identity?
Entra ID, formerly Azure AD, is Microsoft's cloud identity provider for users and apps; Managed Identity gives an Azure resource its own Entra identity.
What problem does Managed Identity solve?
It removes stored credentials — the platform issues and rotates the identity, so the app calls Key Vault, SQL, or Storage with no secret in code.
System-assigned or user-assigned — when would you pick user-assigned?
User-assigned when several resources should share one identity or it must outlive a single resource; system-assigned is tied to and dies with one resource.

followUpsQA.a: System is bound to one resource's lifecycle; user-assigned is standalone and shareable.
The platform issues and rotates tokens, so no credential lives in code or config.
Any Entra-protected service via RBAC — Key Vault, SQL, Storage, etc.
Microsoft's cloud IdP (formerly Azure AD) for OAuth/OIDC sign-in and tokens.

questions.q: Give an overview of ASP.NET Core Identity.

answer.oneLiner: ASP.NET Core Identity is the built-in membership system that comes with the framework for managing users, passwords, roles, and authentication for applications that handle their own local accounts. It provides all the plumbing you would otherwise have to build yourself: secure password hashing using a salted adaptive algorithm, account lockout after failed attempts, two-factor authentication, email confirmation, and external login providers like Google or Microsoft. It is backed by Entity Framework Core by default, so it creates its own schema in your database to store users, roles, and claims. You would typically use it for first-party local account management, and switch to an external identity provider like Entra ID when you need single sign-on across multiple apps or enterprise federation.
keyPoints: ASP.NET Core Identity handles registration, login, and password management out of the box, using a salted adaptive hash such as PBKDF2 for password storage so you do not have to implement password security yourself.
It supports account lockout after configurable failed login attempts, two-factor authentication, and email confirmation for new accounts.
It integrates with external login providers via OAuth 2.0, so users can sign in with Google, Microsoft, or other providers while their local account in your app links to that external identity.
The default storage is Entity Framework Core, which creates a set of tables for users, roles, claims, and tokens in your database.

When you need single sign-on across multiple applications, enterprise federation, or a large-scale consumer identity solution, an external identity provider like Entra ID is a better choice than managing local accounts with Identity.
redFlags: Rolling your own user/password store unnecessarily

dialogue.text: Give me an overview of ASP.NET Core Identity.
It's the built-in membership system for users, passwords, roles, and sign-in, backed by EF Core by default.
What does it handle for you out of the box?
Registration, login, password hashing, lockout, 2FA, external logins, and email confirmation.
When would you skip it for an external IdP?
When I need SSO across apps, social/enterprise federation, or B2C scale — then Entra ID or another OIDC provider beats managing local accounts myself.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use an external IdP instead?
followUpsQA.a: For SSO, federation, or B2C scale rather than first-party local accounts.
With a salted, adaptive hash (PBKDF2) — you don't roll your own.
EF Core by default for users, roles, and claims.
Yes — plus external logins and email confirmation.

questions.q: How do you revoke JWTs given they're stateless?

answer.oneLiner: You cannot truly invalidate a stateless JWT once it is issued — it is self-contained and valid until the expiry time you embedded in it. The practical strategies are to keep access tokens very short-lived so the valid window is small, combined with one of two approaches for forced revocation: a server-side denylist keyed on the token's unique identifier that you check on every request, or a per-user security stamp or token version number embedded in the token that you increment when you want to invalidate all that user's tokens. The denylist gives immediate revocation but reintroduces server state, while the security stamp approach is lighter but still requires a database lookup per request.
keyPoints: JWTs are self-contained and cryptographically signed, so once issued they are valid until they expire — there is no built-in revocation mechanism.
The primary mitigation is to keep access tokens short-lived, typically five to fifteen minutes, so that even a stolen token becomes useless quickly.

For forced immediate revocation, such as when a user logs out or changes their password, you can maintain a server-side denylist of token identifiers that have been invalidated before their expiry, and check it on every request.

An alternative to a denylist is embedding a per-user token version or security stamp in the token claims. When you increment the stamp, all previously issued tokens fail validation because their embedded stamp no longer matches the current value.
Rotating the signing key is a global kill-switch that immediately invalidates every token in circulation, but it affects all users simultaneously so it is only appropriate for emergency scenarios.
redFlags: Claiming you can instantly revoke a stateless JWT
Hour-long access tokens with no revocation

dialogue.text: JWTs are stateless — how do you revoke one?
You can't truly invalidate a self-contained token, so I keep access tokens short-lived and revoke at the refresh step.
What if you need an immediate forced logout?
A server-side denylist keyed on `jti`, or bumping a per-user token version / security stamp so existing tokens fail validation.
Trade-off of a denylist versus just short expiry?
A denylist gives instant revocation but reintroduces server state and a lookup on every request; short expiry stays stateless but leaves a small valid window.

followUpsQA.a: A per-user value that, when changed, invalidates all previously issued tokens.

followUpsQA.q: Trade-off of a denylist vs short expiry?
followUpsQA.a: Denylist = instant revoke but stateful lookups; short expiry = stateless but a small valid window.
At the refresh step — stop issuing new access tokens.
Rotate the signing key so every existing token fails verification.

questions.q: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption, and where is each used?

answer.oneLiner: Symmetric encryption uses a single shared key to both encrypt and decrypt data — it is fast and efficient, which makes it suitable for encrypting large amounts of data like files or database contents. Asymmetric encryption uses a mathematically linked key pair where data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the private key — it is slower and impractical for bulk data, but it solves the key distribution problem because the public key can be shared openly. In practice you use both together: TLS, for example, uses asymmetric cryptography to securely agree on a shared session key, then switches to symmetric encryption for the actual data transfer because it is much faster.
keyPoints: Symmetric encryption uses one key that both encrypts and decrypts. AES is the standard algorithm. It is fast and suitable for encrypting bulk data such as files, database columns, and disk volumes.

The main weakness of symmetric encryption is key distribution — both parties need to share the same secret key, and doing so securely over a network is difficult without another layer of cryptography.

Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair. The public key can be shared freely. Data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted by the corresponding private key. RSA and ECC are common asymmetric algorithms.
Asymmetric encryption is used for key exchange, digital signatures, and TLS certificate authentication rather than bulk data because it is computationally much slower than symmetric encryption.

TLS combines both: it uses asymmetric cryptography during the handshake phase to authenticate the server and securely agree on a symmetric session key, then uses that symmetric key for the rest of the connection because it is far more efficient.
redFlags: Saying asymmetric replaces symmetric for bulk data

dialogue.text: Symmetric vs asymmetric encryption — and where each fits?
Symmetric uses one shared key and is fast for bulk data; asymmetric uses a public/private pair and is slower, used for key exchange and signatures.
TLS — which does it use?
Both: asymmetric to authenticate and agree on a shared key, then symmetric for the actual session traffic because it's fast.
Why not just encrypt everything with RSA and skip symmetric?
RSA is far slower and size-limited per operation; it's impractical for bulk data, which is exactly why TLS hands off to a symmetric key.

followUpsQA.a: Asymmetric to safely agree a key, symmetric for fast bulk encryption of the session.

followUpsQA.q: Why not encrypt everything with RSA?
followUpsQA.a: It's slow and size-limited — unworkable for large payloads.
Securely distributing the shared key.
AES (symmetric); RSA/ECC (asymmetric).

questions.q: What's the difference between hashing and encryption?

answer.oneLiner: Encryption is a two-way process where you use a key to transform data into ciphertext and can later reverse it back to plaintext using that key — it is for protecting data that you need to read again. Hashing is a one-way process that transforms input data into a fixed-length fingerprint, and it is designed to be irreversible — there is no key and no way to get the original input back from the hash. You use hashing for things like storing passwords, because you never need to recover the actual password — you just hash what the user types at login and compare it to the stored hash. A third thing people sometimes confuse with these is encoding such as base64, which is just a way of representing data in a different format and provides no security at all.
keyPoints: Encryption is reversible and requires a key. You use it when you need to store or transmit data confidentially and retrieve the original value later, such as encrypting a connection string or sensitive field in a database.

Hashing is a one-way function that produces a fixed-length output. Given a hash you cannot work backwards to the original input, which makes it appropriate for storing passwords and verifying data integrity.
When storing passwords, you store the hash and compare hashes at login — the server never needs to know the actual password, only whether the hash of what the user typed matches what was stored.
Encoding, such as base64, is not a security measure at all — it is just a transformation of data into a different representation, and anyone can reverse it without any secret.
A common mistake is referring to hashing as encryption or treating base64 as a security mechanism — an interviewer will pick up on this immediately.
redFlags: Calling hashing 'encryption you can't undo' loosely

dialogue.text: Hashing versus encryption?
Encryption is reversible with a key; hashing is a one-way fingerprint you can't reverse.
When do you use each?
Encryption for confidentiality of data you must read back; hashing for integrity checks and storing passwords.
A dev says base64 'encrypts' the payload — your reaction?
Base64 is encoding, not security — it's trivially reversible with no key, so it provides no confidentiality at all.

followUpsQA.a: Encryption for reversible confidentiality; hashing for integrity and passwords.
No — it's reversible encoding, not encryption.
No — one-way is the whole point.

followUpsQA.q: How is hashing used for passwords?
followUpsQA.a: Store a salted slow hash, compare hashes on login.

questions.q: How should you store user passwords?

answer.oneLiner: Passwords must never be stored in plaintext and must not be hashed with a general-purpose fast algorithm like MD5 or SHA-256 — those are designed to be quick, which means an attacker can try billions of guesses per second against a stolen hash file. Instead you use a slow, adaptive algorithm like bcrypt, PBKDF2, or Argon2 that is specifically designed for password hashing and has a configurable work factor you can increase over time as hardware gets faster. Each password also gets its own unique random salt before hashing, which means that two users with the same password will produce completely different hashes, defeating precomputed rainbow table attacks. In ASP.NET Core applications that use Identity, this is all handled for you automatically.
keyPoints: Never store passwords in plaintext — if your database is compromised, every user's password is immediately exposed and every other site where they reused it becomes vulnerable too.

General-purpose hashing algorithms like MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 are designed to be fast, which makes them unsuitable for passwords because an attacker with a GPU can compute billions of hashes per second when cracking a stolen database.

Use a slow, adaptive password hashing algorithm such as bcrypt, PBKDF2, or Argon2. These algorithms have a configurable work factor that you can tune upwards as hardware improves, keeping brute-force attacks impractically slow.

Every password gets a unique random salt — a random value generated at registration time that is included in the hash computation and stored alongside the hash. This ensures that identical passwords produce different hashes, making precomputed dictionary attacks impossible.
ASP.NET Core Identity handles all of this correctly out of the box using PBKDF2, so if you are using the framework's membership system you do not need to implement any of this yourself.

dialogue.text: How should you store user passwords?
Never plaintext and never a fast hash — use a slow, salted, adaptive algorithm like bcrypt, PBKDF2, or Argon2.
Why is a fast hash like SHA-256 a bad choice here?
It's too fast — attackers can try billions of guesses per second; adaptive hashes add a tunable work factor to slow that down.
Two users pick the same password — why won't the stored hashes match?
Because each gets a unique random salt, so identical passwords hash differently, defeating precomputed rainbow tables.

followUpsQA.q: Why is a fast hash like SHA-256 bad here?
followUpsQA.a: Speed lets attackers brute-force billions of guesses per second.
Rainbow-table attacks and identical-password hash collisions.
bcrypt, PBKDF2, or Argon2 — slow and adaptive.

followUpsQA.q: Do you need to build this yourself?
followUpsQA.a: No — ASP.NET Core Identity handles it.

questions.q: How do you manage application secrets and connection strings?

answer.oneLiner: The golden rule is that secrets must never go into source control — no connection strings, API keys, or passwords in appsettings.json or anywhere else in the repository. In a production Azure environment you store secrets in Azure Key Vault and your application reads them at startup using its Managed Identity, so there is no stored credential of any kind in the app's configuration. For local development you use dotnet user-secrets, which stores values in a file outside the project directory where they cannot be accidentally committed. In CI and CD pipelines you use the platform's built-in secret variables which are masked in logs and injected as environment variables at runtime.
keyPoints: Never commit secrets to source control — not in appsettings.json, not in commented-out code, not in any tracked file. Even if the repository is private, git history persists and access can change.

In production on Azure, use Azure Key Vault to store secrets and grant the application's Managed Identity read access to the specific secrets it needs. The app reads them at startup with no stored credentials.

The DefaultAzureCredential class in the Azure SDK automatically uses the Managed Identity when running in Azure and falls back to developer credentials on a local machine, so the same code works in both environments without any conditional logic.
For local development, use dotnet user-secrets to store secrets in a file in your user profile directory, which is outside the project folder and cannot be accidentally staged and committed.

In CI and CD pipelines, use the platform's secret variable mechanism — in Azure DevOps or GitHub Actions, secrets are stored encrypted and injected as environment variables at runtime, and the runner automatically masks them in log output.

code.note: DefaultAzureCredential uses Managed Identity in Azure, dev creds locally.

redFlags: Secrets in plaintext appsettings in prod

dialogue.text: How do you manage secrets and connection strings?
Keep them out of source control — Azure Key Vault via Managed Identity in production, and `dotnet user-secrets` locally, injected at runtime.
How does the app read Key Vault without its own credential?
`DefaultAzureCredential` uses the Managed Identity in Azure and developer creds locally, so there's no stored secret to fetch the secrets.
How do you stop secrets leaking into CI/CD logs?
Use the platform's secret variables / secure store and mark them masked, and never echo them — the runner redacts them from output.

followUpsQA.q: How do you avoid secrets in CI logs?
followUpsQA.a: Use masked secret variables and never echo them; the runner redacts them.
The need for any stored credential to authenticate to Azure services.
`dotnet user-secrets`, outside the repo.
Uses Managed Identity in Azure, dev credentials locally.

questions.q: What's the difference between protecting data at rest and in transit?

answer.oneLiner: Data in transit is data moving across a network, and you protect it with TLS and HTTPS so that anyone intercepting the network traffic sees only encrypted ciphertext. Data at rest is data stored on disk, in a database, or in blob storage, and you protect it with encryption at the storage layer so that anyone who physically steals the disk or a backup file cannot read the data. These are two completely separate threats and two separate controls — using HTTPS does not protect your database backups if someone steals them from a server room, and encrypting your database files on disk does nothing to protect data that is being exfiltrated via SQL injection through a legitimate query.
keyPoints: Data in transit protection means using TLS 1.2 or higher for all network communications so that traffic cannot be read or tampered with by someone intercepting it on the network.

Data at rest protection covers encrypting stored data using mechanisms like Transparent Data Encryption for SQL Server databases, disk-level encryption for virtual machine disks, and storage service encryption for Azure Blob Storage.

You need both controls because they protect against different attack vectors — an attacker sniffing network traffic needs in-transit protection, while an attacker who steals a backup disk needs at-rest encryption.

At-rest encryption is transparent to application-level attacks — a SQL injection attack still reaches the database through legitimate query channels and sees decrypted data, so it does not substitute for preventing injection.
Encryption keys for both in-transit and at-rest scenarios should be managed in a dedicated key management service such as Azure Key Vault rather than stored alongside the data they protect.
redFlags: Assuming HTTPS covers stored data too

dialogue.text: Data at rest versus in transit — distinguish them.
In transit is data moving over the network, protected by TLS/HTTPS; at rest is stored data, protected by disk, DB, or column encryption.
Do you really need both?
Yes — they cover different threats; TLS won't help if someone steals a backup, and disk encryption won't help an attacker sniffing the wire.
Does at-rest encryption stop SQL injection?
No — the database decrypts transparently for legitimate queries, so an injection still reads plaintext; at-rest protects stolen media, not query-level attacks.

followUpsQA.q: Does at-rest encryption stop SQL injection?
followUpsQA.a: No — the DB serves decrypted data to valid queries; injection still reads it.
Transparent Data Encryption — encrypts DB files at rest without app changes.
TLS 1.2+/HTTPS, plus mTLS internally.
In a KMS / Key Vault.

questions.q: How do you isolate data in a multi-tenant application?

answer.oneLiner: Multi-tenant data isolation is about choosing the right point on the spectrum between cost-efficiency and isolation strength. The strongest approach is to give each tenant their own separate database, which means a breach of one tenant's data cannot affect others, but it is the most expensive to provision and manage at scale. At the other end you put all tenants in a single shared schema and distinguish their records with a tenant identifier column, which is the cheapest and most scalable approach but requires you to be rigorous about filtering every query. A critical mistake in the shared-schema model is relying on developers to manually add the tenant filter to every query — instead you enforce it centrally using an Entity Framework global query filter so it is applied automatically and cannot be forgotten.
keyPoints: The three main isolation models are database-per-tenant, schema-per-tenant within a shared server, and a fully shared schema with a tenant identifier column — each represents a different point on the tradeoff between isolation strength and operational cost.

Database-per-tenant provides the strongest isolation because each tenant's data is completely separate, making cross-tenant leaks structurally impossible, but managing hundreds or thousands of separate databases is expensive and operationally complex.
Shared-schema with a tenant identifier is the most cost-efficient model but requires that every single query correctly filters by the current tenant — a single missing filter clause is a data breach.

Never rely on individual developers to remember to add the tenant filter to every query. Use Entity Framework Core global query filters to enforce the filter automatically at the DbContext level so it applies to every query on that entity.

The current tenant identifier must always be resolved server-side from the authenticated user's claims or the verified hostname — never trust a tenant ID that the client sends in the request body, because a malicious user could change it to access another tenant's data.

code.note: A missed WHERE TenantId is a cross-tenant data leak.

redFlags: Relying on developers to remember the tenant filter

dialogue.text: How do you isolate data in a multi-tenant app?
Pick a model on the cost-vs-isolation spectrum: database per tenant, schema per tenant, or a shared schema with a TenantId discriminator.
You go shared-schema — what's the big risk?
A single missing `WHERE TenantId` leaks one tenant's data to another, so I enforce the filter centrally, not per query.
How do you keep that filter from being forgotten or spoofed?
An EF global query filter applies it everywhere automatically, and I set the current tenant server-side from the auth context — never from a client-supplied value.

followUpsQA.a: A forgotten tenant filter cross-leaks data between tenants.

followUpsQA.q: Where do you set the current tenant?
followUpsQA.a: Server-side from the authenticated context, never from client input.

followUpsQA.q: How do you enforce the filter centrally?
followUpsQA.a: EF Core global query filter so every query is scoped automatically.
Database-per-tenant — but costliest to scale.

questions.q: What does handling PII and GDPR basics involve?

answer.oneLiner: Personally identifiable information is any data that can identify a specific individual — names, email addresses, location data, and so on — and handling it responsibly means collecting only what you genuinely need, using it only for the purpose you stated, securing it appropriately, and being able to delete or anonymize it on request. GDPR, the General Data Protection Regulation, is the European Union's legal framework that formalizes these obligations: you must have a lawful basis for processing personal data such as consent or a legitimate interest, you must respect users' rights to access and erase their data, and if you suffer a data breach that involves personal data you must notify the relevant supervisory authority within approximately 72 hours of becoming aware of it.
keyPoints: Data minimization means collecting only the personal data you actually need for a specific stated purpose — storing additional fields just because they might be useful later is not a lawful practice under GDPR.

You must have a lawful basis for processing personal data. Common bases include explicit user consent, contractual necessity where processing is needed to fulfill a contract with the user, and legitimate interest where the processing is necessary for a genuine business reason that does not override the user's rights.

Users have the right to access their data, meaning you must be able to provide a copy of all personal data you hold about them. They also have the right to erasure, meaning you must be able to delete or fully anonymize their data on request.

Pseudonymization means replacing personal identifiers with a key so the data cannot directly identify someone, but it can still be re-linked using that key — pseudonymized data is still personal data under GDPR. Anonymization removes all identifying information permanently and takes the data out of GDPR scope entirely.

A personal data breach that is likely to risk individual rights must be reported to the relevant supervisory authority within roughly 72 hours of you becoming aware of it — this requires having breach detection and incident response procedures in place.
redFlags: No way to delete a user's data

dialogue.text: What does handling PII and GDPR basics involve?
Collect the minimum personal data with a lawful basis, secure it, and honor user rights like access and erasure.
What's the 'right to be forgotten' in practice?
On request you delete or anonymize a user's personal data, which means your design needs a real way to erase it, not just hide it.
A breach exposes PII — what's the clock you're working against?
GDPR generally requires notifying the supervisory authority within about 72 hours of becoming aware, so detection and an incident plan matter.

followUpsQA.q: What is the right to be forgotten?
followUpsQA.a: A user's right to have their personal data deleted or anonymized on request.
Pseudonymized can be re-linked with a key; anonymized cannot — only the latter leaves GDPR scope.
Roughly 72 hours to notify the authority after becoming aware.
Data minimization and purpose limitation — collect only what you need.

questions.q: What is the principle of least privilege and how do you apply it?

answer.oneLiner: The principle of least privilege means that every user, service, and process should have access to only the minimum resources and permissions it needs to do its specific job — nothing more. The practical reason this matters is blast radius: if a component is compromised, the attacker inherits that component's access, so a narrowly-scoped account or token limits how much damage they can do. Applied consistently across database accounts, cloud service permissions, token scopes, and user roles, least privilege turns a single compromised service into a contained incident rather than a full system breach.
keyPoints: The core idea is deny by default, grant explicitly — start with no access and add only the specific permissions needed, rather than starting with broad access and trying to remove what is not needed.

For database accounts, the application should connect with a dedicated account that has only the create, read, update, and delete permissions it actually needs on the specific tables it uses — not db_owner or system administrator privileges.
